items.q: What is the difference between push and pull factors in migration?
items.correct: Push factors drive people away from their origin (war, poverty, persecution, disaster); pull factors attract people to a destination (jobs, safety, family, healthcare, education).
items.misconception: Push and pull factors are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Push factors are physical and pull factors are economic.
items.why: Most migration involves both — people leave AND are attracted somewhere. Pure push (refugees) or pure pull (job offers) is rare. The framework helps but oversimplifies real decisions.

items.q: Why do people move from rural to urban areas in LICs?
items.correct: A combination of push factors (rural poverty, lack of services, agricultural change) and pull factors (perceived jobs, education, healthcare) — though reality often disappoints.
items.misconception: Because cities in poor countries are always better than the countryside.
items.random_wrong: Because the government forces them to move to cities.
items.why: Migrants often end up in shanty towns worse than what they left. The decision is based on perception of opportunity, not guaranteed improvement — a key nuance students miss.

items.q: What happens in Stage 2 of the Demographic Transition Model?
items.correct: Death rates fall rapidly (due to better food, sanitation and medicine) while birth rates remain high, causing rapid population growth.
items.misconception: Birth rates rise rapidly because people want larger families.
items.random_wrong: Both birth and death rates fall sharply in Stage 2.
items.why: The population explosion in Stage 2 is driven by falling deaths, not rising births. Students see growth and assume more babies are being born when in fact more babies are surviving.

items.q: Roughly what % of Japan's population is over 65?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 29%. “10%” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does a wide-based pyramid show?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Youthful population. “Ageing population” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many stages in the Demographic Transition Model?
items.why: The real figure is 5 stages. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
